section.title: Doxology
section.sectionRationale: The letter closes as it opened, with the keeping power of God. After warning of fierce dangers, Jude lifts his readers' eyes to the One who is able to keep them from stumbling and to present them faultless before His glory. This burst of praise is the resolution of everything the letter has said.
section.thesis: The whole fight for the faith rests on the God who alone is able to keep His people from stumbling and to present them blameless with great joy; to Him, our only Savior through Jesus Christ, belongs all glory forever.
section.frameNote: One movement in two beats: the promise of God's keeping and presenting power, verse 24; and the ascription of glory to the only God our Savior through Jesus Christ, verse 25.

verses.text: Now to Him who is able to {{phylasso|keep}} you from {{aptaistos|stumbling}} and to present you {{amomos|unblemished}} in His glorious presence, with great joy,
to the {{monos|only}} God our Savior be glory, majesty, dominion, and authority through Jesus Christ our Lord before all time, and now, and for all eternity. Amen.

phylasso.gloss: To guard, protect, watch over as a sentinel guards what is entrusted to him. God is able to guard His people so that they do not fall away. It is the keeping theme of the whole letter, gathered now into a promise of His protecting power.

aptaistos.gloss: Without stumbling, sure-footed, not falling. The word pictures someone kept from tripping on a dangerous path. Amid the perils of false teaching, God is able to keep His own from the fatal stumble of apostasy and bring them safely through.

amomos.gloss: Blameless, without blemish, faultless. It was used of sacrificial animals fit for God. Believers will be presented not merely acquitted but spotless in His glorious presence, made faultless through Christ, the true unblemished Lamb.

monos.gloss: Only, alone, sole. Against the false teachers' distortions, Jude confesses the only God our Savior. There is one true God, and salvation belongs to Him alone, given through Jesus Christ our Lord, to whom all glory is due.

basic.context: Jude ends with soaring praise. After all the warnings, he points to the One who can actually keep believers safe: God is able to keep you from stumbling and to present you faultless before His glory, and to do it with great joy. Then he gives God every honor, glory, majesty, dominion, and authority, through Jesus Christ, forever. It is the confident, worshipful close to a letter full of danger.
basic.hermeneutics: A doxology is not an add on; it is the point everything has been building toward. Jude has commanded believers to keep themselves, verse 21, and now assures them that God is the one able to keep them, verse 24. Human responsibility rests on divine power. The letter that began with kept in Jesus Christ ends with the God who keeps us from stumbling.
basic.originalLanguages: God is able to phylasso, guard, His people and keep them aptaistos, without stumbling, and to present them amomos, faultless, before His glory. Jude ascribes all glory to the monos, the only, God our Savior, through Jesus Christ our Lord, before all time and forever.
basic.typology: To be presented amomos, unblemished, uses the language of the spotless sacrifice. What the Old Testament required of the offering, God Himself provides for His people in Christ, presenting them faultless, clothed in the perfection of the true unblemished Lamb.
basic.christ: The keeping and presenting are God's, and they come through Jesus Christ our Lord. He is the mediator through whom glory is given to God, and by whom sinners are made faultless. The letter that warned of those who deny our only Master ends by exalting Him as the Lord through whom the only God saves.
basic.god: God is able, He is our Savior, and He is the only God. He does not keep His people grudgingly but presents them with great joy. The whole letter's fearful warnings give way to this: the last word is not the danger of falling but the power and gladness of the God who holds His own.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 16 and other letters close with similar doxologies. First Thessalonians 5 promises that the faithful God will keep believers blameless. And Ephesians 5 speaks of Christ presenting the church without spot or blemish.
basic.oneStory: From the first promise in Eden to the last vision of the redeemed, the Bible tells of a God determined to bring a people safely home. Jude's doxology voices that assurance: the only God our Savior, through Jesus Christ, is able to keep us from stumbling and present us faultless in His presence with great joy, and to Him belongs glory forever.

context.literary: A single, sweeping doxology forming the letter's climax and conclusion. It deliberately answers the opening: the kept of verse 1 are now kept from stumbling in verse 24. The piling up of glory, majesty, dominion, and authority, and the sweep of before all time, now, and forever, give the ending a liturgical, worshipful weight.
context.historical: Doxologies were a familiar way to close letters and prayers in the early church, often spoken aloud in worship. Amen invites the congregation's assent. Jude's ascription of savior to God, through Jesus Christ, reflects the earliest Christian confession that salvation is God's work accomplished in the Son.
context.canonical: This is one of the most beloved doxologies in Scripture, echoing the benedictions of Paul and the worship of the whole Bible. It sets the believer's security not in human effort but in the character and power of God, tying Jude to the canon's grand theme of God preserving His people to the end.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To resolve a letter full of warnings by directing believers' confidence to God's keeping power and by giving Him all glory, so that the readers rest not in their own vigilance but in the Savior who is able to hold them fast.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: A doxology is neither description nor command but praise; yet it functions to assure and to shape response. Its implicit call is to trust the God who keeps and to join in ascribing to Him the glory that is His due.

debates.question: How does God's keeping in verse 24 relate to the command to keep yourselves in verse 21?

views.label: Assurance grounding responsibility
views.case: The command to keep ourselves, verse 21, is real, but it rests entirely on God's prior power to keep us, verse 24. Our perseverance is the fruit of His preserving grace, so the doxology gives rest without cancelling responsibility, as in Philippians 1:6.

views.label: A comforting climax after hard commands
views.case: Others simply note that after strenuous commands Jude ends with comfort: whatever our frailty, God is able, so the final note is confidence, not anxiety.

debates.bearing: Both readings land in the same place: the believer's security is ultimately in God's ability, not our own. We keep ourselves precisely because He is able to keep us, and He will.

debates.question: Who is the Savior in verse 25, and what is the role of Jesus Christ?

views.label: God the only Savior, through the mediation of Christ
views.case: Jude calls God our Savior, and says glory is given through Jesus Christ our Lord. Salvation originates in the one God and is accomplished and mediated through the Son, so both are honored without confusion.

views.label: A unified confession of God and Christ
views.case: The close association of the only God our Savior with Jesus Christ our Lord reflects the high view of Christ found across the New Testament, where the Son shares in the saving work and glory of God.

debates.bearing: Either way the doxology guards monotheism, the only God, while exalting Christ as the Lord through whom that God saves and receives glory, a fitting answer to teachers who denied Him.

study.originalLanguages: The doxology gathers the letter's great theme into praise. God is able to phylasso, guard His people as a sentinel guards a treasure, keeping them aptaistos, sure-footed and unstumbling, on a path lined with danger. He will present them amomos, without blemish, the very word for a sacrifice fit for God, and He will do it not reluctantly but with great joy. All glory belongs to the monos, the only, God our Savior, through Jesus Christ our Lord, spanning before all time, and now, and for all eternity. The letter that opened with believers kept in Christ now closes with the God who is able to keep them from ever finally falling.

typology.type: Being presented unblemished before God's glory
typology.fulfillment: Fulfills the Old Testament requirement of a spotless offering, realized as God presents His people faultless in Christ, the true unblemished Lamb, Ephesians 5:27.

typology.type: The God who keeps His people from stumbling
typology.fulfillment: The consummation of God's covenant faithfulness to preserve His own, fulfilled in Christ who loses none the Father gives Him, John 10:28-29.

study.christ: The doxology exalts God as Savior, yet it is expressly through Jesus Christ our Lord that this glory is rendered and this salvation accomplished. Christ is the mediator through whom the only God keeps and presents His people, and the one who makes them amomos, faultless, by His own spotless sacrifice. The letter that condemned those who deny our only Master and Lord ends by giving Him His rightful place: the Lord through whom the only God our Savior is glorified forever.
study.god: God is revealed here in three glorious notes. He is able, His power is equal to keeping frail believers through every danger. He is Savior, salvation is His own work and gift. And He is the only God, sharing His glory with no idol or pretender. Astonishingly, He keeps and presents His people with great joy, so the last impression of the letter is not the terror of falling but the gladness of a God delighting to bring His own safely home.

crossReferences.note: The God of peace will keep you blameless; He who calls you is faithful and will do it, paralleling Jude's promise.
Christ presents the church to Himself in splendor, without spot or blemish, echoing present you unblemished.
No one can snatch Christ's sheep from His or the Father's hand, the keeping power Jude celebrates.
A closing doxology to the God who is able, giving glory through Jesus Christ forever, closely parallel to Jude's.

study.oneStory: The Bible's long story of a God determined to save and keep a people reaches a triumphant note here. After every warning of danger and falling, Jude fixes our eyes on the only God our Savior, who is able, through Jesus Christ, to keep us from stumbling and to present us faultless in His glorious presence, and to do it with great joy. To that God belongs glory, majesty, dominion, and authority, before all time, now, and forever. Amen.

sermon.title: Now to Him
sermon.why: From Begg's series A Study in Jude, this closing message expounds the doxology of Jude 1:24-25, praising the God who is able to keep His people from stumbling and present them faultless, and how that knowledge shapes worship and mission. A clear, orthodox handling of this movement.

quiz.q: What is the great assurance of verse 24?

options.text: God is able to keep believers from stumbling and to present them faultless before His glory.
options.note: Jude 1:24 places the believer's security in God's keeping and presenting power.

options.text: Believers must keep themselves faultless by their own strength.
options.note: The keeping here is God's ability, not our unaided effort.

options.text: God will present only the most successful believers.
options.note: The promise is for His people, kept by His power, not by their success.

options.text: Stumbling is inevitable and final for everyone.
options.note: The point is the opposite: God is able to keep His own from the fatal fall.

quiz.q: How does the doxology relate to the earlier command to keep yourselves, verse 21?

options.text: God's ability to keep us grounds and secures our own keeping.
options.note: Our perseverance rests on His preserving power, as in Philippians 1:6.

options.text: It cancels the command, so effort no longer matters.
options.note: It grounds the command rather than cancelling it; we keep ourselves because He keeps us.

options.text: It contradicts the command outright.
options.note: There is no contradiction; divine keeping and human keeping work together.

options.text: It means our keeping earns God's keeping.
options.note: His keeping is the ground of ours, not a reward we earn.

quiz.q: What does it mean that God will present believers unblemished?

options.text: Faultless and spotless, using sacrificial language, made perfect through Christ.
options.note: Amomos was used of unblemished offerings; believers are presented spotless in Christ.

options.text: Merely let off without any real transformation.
options.note: It is more than acquittal; they are made truly faultless in His presence.

options.text: Presented only if they never sinned at all.
options.note: It is God's doing through Christ, not a reward for a sinless record.

options.text: Left in their sin but overlooked by God.
options.note: They are made blameless, not merely overlooked.

quiz.q: How does Jude describe God as he gives Him glory in verse 25?

options.text: The only God our Savior, glorified through Jesus Christ our Lord forever.
options.note: Jude 1:25 confesses the one God as Savior, honored through Christ for all eternity.

options.text: One of many gods worthy of some honor.
options.note: Jude confesses the only God, guarding true monotheism.

options.text: A distant God who takes no part in salvation.
options.note: He is expressly our Savior, deeply involved in saving His people.

options.text: A Savior apart from any role for Jesus Christ.
options.note: Glory is given through Jesus Christ our Lord; the Son is central.

quiz.q: What tone does God's keeping carry at the end of the letter?

options.text: Great joy: God delights to present His people faultless before His glory.
options.note: Jude 1:24 says with great joy, ending the letter on gladness, not dread.

options.text: Reluctant duty on God's part.
options.note: The text says great joy, not reluctance.

options.text: Cold indifference toward the outcome.
options.note: Far from indifferent, God rejoices to keep and present His own.

options.text: Anxious uncertainty about whether He can succeed.
options.note: He is able, and He acts with joy; there is no uncertainty.

questions: The letter warned of many dangers, then ended by saying God is able to keep you from stumbling. How does that promise steady you when you feel your own weakness?
You are told both to keep yourselves and that God keeps you. How does trusting His power change the way you pursue perseverance?
God will present His people faultless, and He does it with great joy. How does it move you to know that God delights, not merely consents, to save you?
Jude gives God every honor, glory, majesty, dominion, and authority. What would it look like for your daily life to give God that place in practice?
The letter ends in worship after facing real threats. How can praise become your own response to danger and uncertainty, rather than only fear?

translationNotes.note: Where the BSB prints a dash after with great joy, it has been rendered as a comma per house style, leading into the ascription of verse 25. Keep you from stumbling renders phylasso with aptaistos, guarded so as not to fall.

Some manuscripts add through Jesus Christ our Lord and the fuller list of attributes; the BSB reflects the best attested text. Before all time, and now, and for all eternity renders a sweeping expression of God's everlasting glory.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Jude 1:24-25. The dash in verse 24 has been rendered as a comma per house style.
